questions.question_text: What type of insurance does Tennessee require all real estate licensees to maintain?
distractors: General liability insurance
Professional malpractice insurance
Surety bond coverage
questions.explanation: Tennessee requires all licensees to maintain Errors and Omissions (E&O) insurance. E&O specifically covers claims from professional negligence, errors, or omissions in licensed activities.

questions.question_text: An affiliate broker's E&O insurance policy lapses. What is the consequence?
distractors: A 30-day grace period applies automatically
The broker receives a warning letter only
The affiliate can practice for 60 more days
questions.explanation: E&O insurance is mandatory — no license can be issued or renewed without proof of coverage. A lapse in coverage means the licensee cannot legally practice until coverage is restored.

questions.question_text: How does a Tennessee licensee typically obtain E&O insurance?
distractors: Through the state general fund
Only through private individual policies
Through the Tennessee Recovery Fund
questions.explanation: TREC approves a group E&O policy for licensees. Licensees may also obtain individual policies that meet minimum requirements, but the group policy is the most common method.

questions.question_text: E&O insurance covers which type of claim against a licensee?
distractors: Personal injury on the licensee's property
Damage to a client's vehicle at a showing
Theft of personal property from an office
questions.explanation: E&O insurance specifically covers claims arising from professional negligence, errors, or omissions in the course of licensed real estate activities. It is not general liability insurance.

questions.question_text: What distinguishes E&O insurance from general liability insurance?
distractors: E&O is optional while liability is mandatory
E&O has lower premiums than liability
E&O covers physical injuries at showings
questions.explanation: E&O insurance specifically covers professional mistakes made during licensed activities (e.g., failing to disclose a defect), while general liability covers bodily injury and property damage.

questions.question_text: A new brokerage opens in Nashville. What must the firm do with TREC?
questions.correct_answer: Register and obtain a firm license
distractors: Only the principal broker needs registration
File a notice of intent to operate
Obtain approval from the local board
questions.explanation: Tennessee requires firms to be registered and licensed with TREC. Individual licensee registration alone is not sufficient — the firm entity itself must hold a license.

questions.question_text: A client visits a brokerage office and asks to verify the agents' credentials. Where should licenses be available?
distractors: Only on the TREC website
In each agent's personal files at home
Only upon written request to TREC
questions.explanation: Tennessee requires that real estate licenses be displayed at the brokerage firm's office. This ensures transparency and allows clients and inspectors to verify licensure.

questions.question_text: A principal broker opens a second branch office. What licensing requirement applies?
questions.correct_answer: The branch must be registered with TREC
distractors: No additional registration is needed
A separate principal broker is required
The firm must obtain a new firm license
questions.explanation: Branch offices must be registered with TREC. The principal broker maintains oversight, but each office location must be properly registered to ensure regulatory compliance.

questions.question_text: When TREC audits a firm, which document must be prominently displayed?
distractors: The firm's E&O insurance certificate
The principal broker's diploma
The firm's business tax receipt
questions.explanation: The firm's real estate license must be displayed at the office. While E&O insurance must be maintained, the license itself is the document required to be on display.

questions.question_text: What happens if a firm operates without a proper TREC registration?
questions.correct_answer: The firm is practicing without a license
distractors: Only the principal broker faces penalties
TREC issues a warning for first offense
The firm has 90 days to register
questions.explanation: Operating without proper firm registration constitutes unlicensed practice. Both the firm and its principal broker may face disciplinary action for failing to maintain proper TREC registration.

questions.question_text: A licensee from another state wants to practice in Tennessee. What education must they complete?
questions.correct_answer: The same education required of TN residents
distractors: Only a 30-hour Tennessee-specific course
No education if licensed over 5 years
Half the hours required of TN residents
questions.explanation: Nonresident licensees must meet the same educational criteria as Tennessee residents. There is no reduced education requirement based on out-of-state licensure.

questions.question_text: A nonresident licensee wants to work in Tennessee. With whom must they affiliate?
questions.correct_answer: A firm with a TN-licensed principal broker
distractors: Any firm licensed in their home state
The Tennessee Association of Realtors
A firm registered with the local MLS
questions.explanation: Nonresident licensees must affiliate with a firm licensed to operate in Tennessee that has a TN-licensed principal broker. They cannot operate under their home state broker alone.

questions.question_text: A nonresident applicant previously passed a national exam through a recognized testing company. What may they request?
questions.correct_answer: A waiver of the national exam section
distractors: A waiver of the state exam section
A waiver of all education requirements
Automatic licensure without any exam
questions.explanation: Nonresident licensees may request a waiver of the national section if they passed a national exam administered by a recognized testing company. They must still pass the Tennessee state section.

questions.question_text: A Florida licensee obtains a Tennessee license through the nonresident process. Which section of the TN exam can potentially be waived?
distractors: The state portion only
Both national and state portions
Neither portion can be waived
questions.explanation: A nonresident who previously passed a recognized national exam may request a waiver of the national section only. The Tennessee state section must always be taken to demonstrate knowledge of TN-specific law.

questions.question_text: Can a nonresident licensee practice in Tennessee while supervised by a broker located entirely in another state?
questions.correct_answer: No, a TN-licensed principal broker is required
distractors: Yes, if the other state has reciprocity
Yes, with TREC written approval
Yes, for the first 6 months only
questions.explanation: Nonresident licensees must be affiliated with a firm that has a Tennessee-licensed principal broker. Supervision by an out-of-state-only broker does not meet Tennessee's requirements.
